front: Work: commissioned for the 125th anniversary of the New York Philharmonic; for biwa, shakuhachi, and orchestra
Work: Takemitsu secluded himself with Debussy scores (Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun and Jeux) at a mountain villa to write this work
Work: Seiji Ozawa conducted the premiere of this Takemitsu work
Work: Leonard Bernstein's suggestions after hearing Eclipse inspired Takemitsu to write this work
Work: originally titled 'Water Rings'; Jasper Johns advised Takemitsu to rename it to reflect its premiere month
Work: work for biwa and shakuhachi; long pauses during which poetry by Rabindranath Tagore is recited
Work: hearing this Takemitsu work led Leonard Bernstein to suggest writing November Steps
Work: Takemitsu's last solo piano work; subtitled 'in memoriam Olivier Messiaen'
Work: uses an E-flat–E–A sea motive; dedicated to Messiaen
Work: its many silences exemplify the Japanese concept of ma (negative space); episodes marked 'Celestially light' bookend it
Work: Greenpeace-commissioned piece inspired by Moby Dick; features an S-E-A musical cryptogram
Work: version for flute, harp, and string orchestra; sections each reference Moby Dick
Work: music abruptly stops when a gunshot rings out during the attack on the third castle
Work: second Kurosawa collaboration (after Dodes'ka-den); includes the track 'Hell's Picture Scroll' in a battle scene
Work: includes 'Flute of Darkness' — a shakuhachi plays piercing high notes as buildings burn
Work: aleatoric piece; five etudes represented in the score as circles; no traditional musical notation; influenced by John Cage
Work: string orchestra piece praised by Stravinsky, who took Takemitsu out to lunch after hearing it
Work: heard on Stravinsky's 1958 tour through Tokyo; helped gain Takemitsu international recognition
Work: viola concerto; title taken from a poem by Makota Ooka
Work: subtitled 'Say sea, take me!'; references the influence of Debussy's La mer
Work: written after Takemitsu met choreographer Jiri Kylian and indigenous dancers in Australia's Northern Territory
Work: opens with atonal music over mist swirling around an empty suit of armor; climactic murder set to 'Dido's Lament'
Work: string glissandi represent shifting sands
back: Woman in the Dunes (film score) (Toru Takemitsu)

front: Work: climactic duel in rippling grass set to aggressive biwa strumming; Donald Richie noted composer repeatedly used a single chord fading into silence
Work: for flute, viola, and harp; same trio instrumentation as Debussy's sonata for diverse instruments
back: And then I knew 'twas Wind (Toru Takemitsu)

front: Work: homage to Messiaen's Quartet for the End of Time; written after Messiaen played an excerpt for Takemitsu at the piano
Work: 12 Songs for guitar includes a transcription of Summertime; Takemitsu also wrote Valeria and Folios for guitar
work: Guitar Works / 12 Songs / Valeria / Folios / And then I knew 'twas Wind

front: Composer: largely self-taught modern Japanese composer; called Debussy his 'great mentor' and Messiaen his 'spiritual mentor'; studied under John Cage
Composer: incorporated traditional Japanese instruments biwa and shakuhachi into concert works including Eclipse and November Steps
Composer: collaborated frequently with Akira Kurosawa; composed scores for over 100 films, especially Japanese New Wave cinema
Composer: signature technique of careful use of silence in film scores
Composer: likened his music to 'the stillness of grass and flowers'
Composer: initially averse to Japanese musical tradition; embraced it after a commission for the Imperial Household's gagaku ensemble
Composer: wrote one novel, featuring a detective character
Composer: an annual composition award bears his name; Kaija Saariaho was the first woman to judge it (2015)
